questions.question: Explain the key difference between Randomized Select and QuickSort in terms of recursion strategy.
keyPoints: Randomized Select recurses into only one partition while QuickSort recurses into both
Randomized Select only processes the partition containing the k-th element
This makes Randomized Select approximately twice as fast as QuickSort for selection problems

questions.question: Why does Randomized Select use a random pivot instead of a fixed pivot?
keyPoints: Fixed pivot (e.g., first element) can lead to O(n²) worst-case on sorted input
Random pivot ensures expected O(n) time by probabilistically avoiding unbalanced partitions
Provides good average-case performance without knowing input distribution

questions.question: Derive the expected time complexity O(n) of Randomized Select.
keyPoints: Assume each partition splits are equally likely
Expected work at each level is O(n) for partitioning
On average, the problem size reduces by constant factor
Sum of geometric series gives O(n)

questions.question: Write the recurrence relation for Randomized Select and solve it.
keyPoints: T(n) = T(n/2) + O(n) for the average case
T(n) = T(n-1) + O(n) for worst case
Average case solves to O(n) using Master Theorem
Worst case is O(n²)

questions.question: How would you modify Randomized Select to find the k-th largest element instead of k-th smallest?
keyPoints: Use k' = n - k + 1 to convert to k-th smallest problem
Or directly search for (n-k)th smallest element
The partition logic remains the same, only k value changes

questions.question: Compare Randomized Select with deterministic selection algorithms.
keyPoints: Randomized: O(n) expected, O(n²) worst-case, simpler implementation
Deterministic (Median of Medians): O(n) worst-case, more complex
Randomized is faster in practice due to better constants
Deterministic preferred when worst-case guarantee is critical

questions.question: What are order statistics and how is Randomized Select used to find them?
keyPoints: Order statistics are the k-th smallest (or largest) elements in a set
Examples: minimum (k=1), maximum (k=n), median (k=n/2)
Randomized Select efficiently finds any order statistic in O(n) average time
Useful for finding quartiles, percentiles without full sorting

questions.question: Describe the Randomized Select algorithm in detail. Trace through an example array [7, 2, 9, 3, 5, 1, 8] to find the 4th smallest element, showing each partition step.
keyPoints: Algorithm: Select random pivot, partition array around it
After partition, compare pivot index with k-1
If pivot index > k-1, recurse left; if < k-1, recurse right with adjusted k
Trace: [7,2,9,3,5,1,8], random pivot (say 5), partition to [3,1,5,7,2,9,8]
Pivot index=2 (value 5), k=4, so go right with new k=4-2=2
Continue until pivot index = k-1 = 3, return element

questions.question: Prove that the expected time complexity of Randomized Select is O(n). Include the recurrence derivation and solution.
keyPoints: Define T(n) as expected time for n elements
T(n) ≤ T(3n/4) + cn with probability 1/2 (unbalanced)
T(n) ≤ T(n/2) + cn with probability 1/2 (balanced)
Expected value: T(n) ≤ (1/2)(T(3n/4) + cn) + (1/2)(T(n/2) + cn)
Solve using substitution or recursion tree method
Total expected work forms a geometric series: cn(1 + 3/4 + (3/4)² + ...) = O(n)

questions.question: Write a complete pseudocode for Randomized Select and analyze its best, average, and worst-case time and space complexities.
keyPoints: Pseudocode: RANDOMIZED-SELECT(A, p, r, i)
If p == r, return A[p]
q = RANDOMIZED-PARTITION(A, p, r)
k = q - p + 1
If i == k, return A[q]
Else if i < k, return RANDOMIZED-SELECT(A, p, q-1, i)
Else return RANDOMIZED-SELECT(A, q+1, r, i-k)
Best: O(n), Average: O(n), Worst: O(n²)
Space: O(log n) average, O(n) worst due to recursion

questions.question: Compare Randomized Select with: (a) Sorting the entire array, (b) Using a Min-Heap, (c) Deterministic Selection (Median of Medians). Discuss time complexity, space usage, and when to prefer each.
keyPoints: (a) Sorting: O(n log n) time, O(1)/O(n) space, use when multiple order stats needed
(b) Min-Heap: O(n + k log n), use when finding smallest k elements
(c) Deterministic: O(n) worst-case, more complex constants, use when guarantee needed
Randomized Select: O(n) expected, simple, best for single order statistic in practice
Choice depends on: whether worst-case guarantee needed, k value, number of queries
